category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Similar Questions/Part B/Probability — Combinatorial Probability

questiontext.text: In a group of \(n\) people, the probability that at least two share the same birthday (365 days, uniform distribution) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(1-\dfrac{365\cdot364\cdots(365-n+1)}{365^n}\)

P(all different birthdays) = \(\frac{365}{365}\cdot\frac{364}{365}\cdots\frac{365-n+1}{365}=\frac{365!}{(365-n)!\cdot365^n}\). So P(at least two match) = \(1-P(\text{all different})\). For \(n=23\): this probability exceeds 1/2. The birthday paradox surprises most people.

questiontext.text: Two students each independently mark 30 MCQs at random (each with 4 options). The probability that all their 30 answers coincide is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(4^{-30}\)

For each question, student 1 picks one of 4 answers uniformly, student 2 picks one of 4. Probability they agree on one question: \(1/4\). By independence over 30 questions: \((1/4)^{30}=4^{-30}\).

questiontext.text: A box has 5 red and 3 blue balls. Picking 3 without replacement, the probability of getting exactly 2 red balls is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(15/28\)

Hypergeometric: \(P(X=2)=\dfrac{\binom{5}{2}\binom{3}{1}}{\binom{8}{3}}=\dfrac{10\cdot3}{56}=\dfrac{30}{56}=\dfrac{15}{28}\). This is the hypergeometric distribution with \(N=8,K=5,n=3\), computing the probability that exactly 2 of the 3 drawn are red.

Answer: (A) \(0.8\)

Inclusion-exclusion: \(P(A\cup B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A\cap B)=0.6+0.5-0.3=0.8\). Verify: since \(P(A\cup B)\leq1\): \(0.8\leq1\) ✓. Also note that independence would give \(P(A\cap B)=0.3=0.6\times0.5=0.3\) — so here \(A\) and \(B\) are actually independent.

questiontext.text: A test for a disease is 95% sensitive (P(positive|disease)=0.95) and 90% specific (P(negative|no disease)=0.90). The disease prevalence is 1%. Given a positive test, the probability of having the disease is approximately:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) 8.7%

Bayes: \(P(D|+)=\frac{P(+|D)P(D)}{P(+)}=\frac{0.95\times0.01}{0.95\times0.01+0.10\times0.99}=\frac{0.0095}{0.0095+0.099}=\frac{0.0095}{0.1085}\approx0.0876\approx8.7\%\). This illustrates how low prevalence makes positive tests mostly false positives — a counter-intuitive result important in medical statistics.

questiontext.text: Three students each independently and uniformly mark 30 MCQs (4 options each). The probability that all three give identical answer sheets is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) \(4^{-60}\)

Fix student 1's answer sheet. Student 2 must match all 30 answers: probability \((1/4)^{30}=4^{-30}\). Student 3 must also match all 30: probability \(4^{-30}\). These are independent. Total probability: \(4^{-30}\times4^{-30}=4^{-60}\).

questiontext.text: A fair coin is tossed 10 times. The probability of getting exactly 6 heads is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\binom{10}{6}(1/2)^{10}=105/512\)

Binomial: \(P(X=6)=\binom{10}{6}(1/2)^6(1/2)^4=\binom{10}{6}/2^{10}=210/1024=105/512\approx0.205\). The binomial distribution \(B(n,p)\) models the number of successes in \(n\) independent Bernoulli trials with success probability \(p\).

questiontext.text: A fair die is rolled until a 6 appears. The expected number of rolls is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) 6

Geometric distribution with \(p=1/6\): \(E[X]=1/p=6\). More generally, the geometric distribution models the number of trials until the first success, with \(E[X]=1/p\). The PMF is \(P(X=k)=(1-p)^{k-1}p\) for \(k=1,2,\ldots\)

questiontext.text: If \(X\sim\text{Poisson}(\lambda=3)\), then \(P(X=0)=\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(e^{-3}\)

Poisson PMF: \(P(X=k)=e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k/k!\). For \(k=0\): \(P(X=0)=e^{-\lambda}=e^{-3}\approx0.0498\). The Poisson distribution models rare events in a fixed time interval (calls to a switchboard, radioactive decays, etc.).

questiontext.text: Two dice are rolled. Given that the sum is at least 10, the probability that both show the same number is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(1/6\)

Sum \(\geq10\): pairs \((4,6),(5,5),(5,6),(6,4),(6,5),(6,6)\) — that's 6 outcomes. Among these, pairs with equal numbers: only \((5,5)\) — 1 outcome. Conditional probability: \(1/6\).

name.text: Probability - Random Walk Returns to Origin

questiontext.text: In a symmetric random walk on \(\mathbb{Z}\) (equal probability \(1/2\) left or right), the probability of returning to the origin is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) 1 (certain to return)

Recurrence of simple random walk on \(\mathbb{Z}\): the probability of returning to the origin is 1 (Pólya's theorem: in 1D and 2D, the walk is recurrent; in 3D, it is transient with probability \(\approx0.34\) of never returning). In 1D: \(P(\text{return})=1\) because \(\sum_{n=1}^\infty P(\text{at origin at step } 2n)=\infty\).

questiontext.text: The number of ways to distribute 12 distinct objects into 3 distinct boxes so that box 1 gets 4, box 2 gets 4, and box 3 gets 4 is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\dfrac{12!}{4!4!4!}=34650\)

Multinomial coefficient: \(\binom{12}{4,4,4}=\frac{12!}{4!4!4!}=\frac{479001600}{24\cdot24\cdot24}=\frac{479001600}{13824}=34650\). More generally, the multinomial theorem: \((x_1+\cdots+x_k)^n=\sum\binom{n}{n_1,\ldots,n_k}x_1^{n_1}\cdots x_k^{n_k}\).

questiontext.text: The number of derangements (permutations with no fixed points) of \(n\) elements is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(D_n=n!\sum_{k=0}^n\frac{(-1)^k}{k!}\)

Derangement formula: \(D_n=n!\left(1-\frac{1}{1!}+\frac{1}{2!}-\cdots+\frac{(-1)^n}{n!}\right)\). For large \(n\): \(D_n/n!\to e^{-1}\approx0.368\). Probability of a random permutation being a derangement \(\approx1/e\). Derived via inclusion-exclusion over the events "position \(i\) is a fixed point".

questiontext.text: A biased coin with \(P(\text{H})=p\) is tossed until the \(r\)-th head appears. The mean number of tosses is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(r/p\)

Negative binomial distribution: waiting for the \(r\)-th success, each with probability \(p\). Mean \(=r/p\). For \(r=1\): geometric distribution with mean \(1/p\). The PMF: \(P(X=k)=\binom{k-1}{r-1}p^r(1-p)^{k-r}\) for \(k=r,r+1,\ldots\)

questiontext.text: Events \(A\) and \(B\) are independent if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(P(A\cap B)=P(A)\cdot P(B)\)

Independence: \(P(A\cap B)=P(A)P(B)\). Equivalently: \(P(A|B)=P(A)\) (knowledge of \(B\) doesn't affect probability of \(A\)). Three events are mutually independent if all pairs and the triple product condition hold. Independence is stronger than pairwise independence.

questiontext.text: Chebyshev's inequality: for a random variable \(X\) with mean \(\mu\) and variance \(\sigma^2\), the probability \(P(|X-\mu|\geq k\sigma)\leq\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(1/k^2\)

Chebyshev: \(P(|X-\mu|\geq k\sigma)\leq1/k^2\) for any \(k>0\). More generally, \(P(|X-\mu|\geq t)\leq\sigma^2/t^2\). This holds for ANY distribution with finite variance — no assumption of normality. For \(k=2\): at least 75% of data within 2 standard deviations of the mean.

questiontext.text: From a group of 10 (6 men, 4 women), 3 are selected with replacement. Probability all 3 are women:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \((2/5)^3=8/125\)

With replacement, each selection is independent. \(P(\text{woman})=4/10=2/5\). \(P(\text{all 3 women})=(2/5)^3=8/125=0.064\).

questiontext.text: If \(X\sim N(0,1)\), then \(P(|X|>1.96)\approx\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) 0.05

Standard normal: \(P(|Z|>1.96)=2P(Z>1.96)\approx2\times0.025=0.05\). This is the famous critical value: 1.96 is the 97.5th percentile of the standard normal. Used in 95% confidence intervals: \(\bar{X}\pm1.96\sigma/\sqrt{n}\). Also \(P(|Z|>2.576)\approx0.01\) (99% CI).

questiontext.text: Using Stirling's approximation \(n!\approx\sqrt{2\pi n}(n/e)^n\), the central binomial coefficient \(\binom{2n}{n}\) is approximately:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\dfrac{4^n}{\sqrt{\pi n}}\)

\(\binom{2n}{n}=\frac{(2n)!}{(n!)^2}\approx\frac{\sqrt{4\pi n}(2n/e)^{2n}}{2\pi n(n/e)^{2n}}=\frac{\sqrt{4\pi n}\cdot4^n\cdot n^{2n}/e^{2n}}{2\pi n\cdot n^{2n}/e^{2n}}=\frac{2\sqrt{\pi n}\cdot4^n}{2\pi n}=\frac{4^n}{\sqrt{\pi n}}\). This is the asymptotic formula for the central binomial coefficient.

questiontext.text: Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be independent discrete uniform random variables on \(\{1,2,3,4\}\). The probability \(P(X=Y)\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(1/4\)

\(P(X=Y)=\sum_{k=1}^4P(X=k)P(Y=k)=\sum_{k=1}^4\frac{1}{4}\cdot\frac{1}{4}=4\cdot\frac{1}{16}=\frac{1}{4}\). By independence, the sum over all matching values gives \(1/n\) for a discrete uniform on \(\{1,\ldots,n\}\).
